63	
64	# Blueprint Writer
65	
66	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
67	
68	## Your Job
69	
70	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
71	
72	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
73	
74	## Directive Format
75	
76	```markdown
77	# Blueprint Writer Directive
78	
79	## Slug
80	<slug>
81	
82	## Target chapter
83	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
84	
85	## Strategy context
86	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
87	
88	## Required content
89	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
90	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
91	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
92	- ...
93	
94	## Out of scope
95	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
96	
97	## References
98	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
99	- ...
100	
101	## Expected outcome
102	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
103	```
104	
105	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
106	
107	## Chapter format
108	
109	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
110	
111	```latex
112	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
113	  \label{thm:some_label}
114	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
115	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
116	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
117	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
118	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
119	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
120	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
121	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
122	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
123	\end{theorem}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
126	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
127	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
128	\begin{proof}
129	  \uses{thm:another_result}
130	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
131	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
132	\end{proof}
133	```
134	
135	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
136	
137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
144	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
145	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
146	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
147	
148	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
149	
150	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
151	
152	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
153	
154	**The hard rule, explicit:**
155	
156	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
157	
158	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
159	
160	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
161	
162	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
163	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
164	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
165	
166	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.
167	
168	## Rules
169	
170	### What you CAN do
171	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
172	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
173	- **Add, correct, or complete `\uses{...}` cross-references** so the dependency edges match the real mathematics. If your chapter's proof of `T` invokes lemma `L`, `T` must declare `\uses{lem:L}`; if a `\uses{}` points at a label that no longer exists, fix it. Verify with `leandag` (see "Verifying dependencies with leandag" below) — do not eyeball it.
174	- Adjust `\lean{...}` hints when the directive names a new Lean target.
175	- **Write entries for Lean helper declarations.** The project keeps a 1-to-1 Lean ↔ blueprint correspondence: when the directive lists a helper that exists only in Lean (a prover-created lemma, an instance, a decomposition step), give it a real entry — concise informal statement, `\label{}`, `\lean{}` with the exact Lean name, accurate `\uses{}`, and at least a one-line informal proof. "It's trivial" is not a reason to skip it: the entry is what carries the dependency edges and what helps a later prover fill the sorry. When the helper's Lean proof relies on a fact with no blueprint entry, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" (or create it too, if it belongs in your chapter).
176	- **Author a *Mathlib dependency anchor* and mark it `\mathlibok`** when your chapter relies on a result Mathlib already provides — write out the statement and point `\lean{}` at the real Mathlib declaration (see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below). This is the only marker you may add.
177	- **Remove a declaration block ONLY when the directive explicitly lists it for removal** (typically an isolated/orphaned statement the reviewer flagged `remove`). Default to wiring an isolated node into the graph (add the missing `\uses{}`), not deleting it — an isolated node is usually a missing edge, not dead weight. Never remove the project goal, and never remove a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
178	- Read `references/summary.md` and any reference that is in `references/` to ground your writing in the project's sources.
179	
180	### What you MUST do
181	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX.** Don't leave dangling `\begin{...}` without matching `\end{...}`. Compile-checking is the plan agent's responsibility but you must not introduce syntax errors.
182	- **Stay within your chapter.** Your declared write-domain is one `*.tex` file. The Archon CLI rejects writes outside it.
183	- **Define non-standard macros in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`** before using them — but: that file is outside your write-domain, so you DON'T touch it. If the directive requires a new macro, you note in your report "needs macro `\foo`" and leave the LaTeX using the new command name; the plan agent adds the macro before next iter's typeset.
184	- **Use mathematical, not Lean-syntactic prose.** Describe the proof in the language of mathematics — definitions, set inclusions, ring maps, universal properties — not in Lean tactic syntax. The prover formalizes your math.
185	- **Follow citation discipline** for every block derived from external reference material (see "Citation discipline (the hard rule)" above). Each such block needs `% SOURCE:` with a local-file parenthetical, `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with a verbatim original-language quote, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof env when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: ...}` prefix. Never cite from memory — only from a local `references/<file>.md` you opened and read in this session.
186	- **Document every change** in your report, including which `references/<file>.md` files you opened (under "References consulted").
187	
188	### What you MUST NOT do
189	- **Do NOT reference project history.** No "since iteration N," "after our failed attempt," or session narrative. The blueprint must read as a standalone mathematical document.
190	- **Do NOT include Lean implementation details.** No tactic suggestions, typeclass wiring notes, or Lean code structure hints in the prose. If a Lean note is needed, it belongs in `.lean` files (the plan agent will use `lean-scaffolder` for that).
191	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` markers.** `\leanok` is earned by a sorry-free Lean proof and set by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase — never by you. (`\mathlibok` is different: you MAY add it, but ONLY on a Mathlib dependency anchor — see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below — never on the project's own to-be-proved declarations.)
192	- **Do NOT edit other chapters.** Even when you spot a related issue, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" instead of fixing it.
193	- **Do NOT edit `content.tex`** (the top-level blueprint file that `\input`s the chapters).
194	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
195	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
196	- **Do NOT write literal `REF` placeholders.** Never produce prose like "Definition~REF" or "Sections REF–REF". Cross-reference declarations with `\cref{<label>}` using the real label (look it up in the chapter or via `leandag`); when the target genuinely has no label yet, describe it by name in prose. The blueprint-doctor flags every literal `REF` as a malformed reference.
197	- **Do NOT interleave math delimiters.** Pick `\( … \)` for a formula and close it with `\)` — never produce `$ … \( … \) … $` or `X\(, hence \)Y` inversions; the doctor's `math-delim` lint flags every such site and the rendered output shreds mid-formula.
198	- **Do NOT paste bare label ids into prose.** "Thm.~th:main" is not a reference — write `\cref{...}` for project declarations, or the source's human-readable number ("Thm.~4.8") when quoting external material.
199	- **Do NOT use undefined macros.** Every `\foo` your prose needs must exist in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` or be `\providecommand`'d at the top of your chapter (chapter-local is fine; report a "needs macro" note for shared ones).
200	- **Do NOT touch protected blocks.** Your invocation prompt's "Protected by the mathematician" section lists `archon-protected.yaml` rules. A `statement`-protected label's declaration block (statement + `\label`/`\lean`/`\uses`) is frozen — you may still write its `\begin{proof}`; an `all`-protected label is entirely off-limits. You may freely `\uses{}` protected labels from other blocks. If your directive conflicts with a protection rule, stop and report the conflict instead of editing.
201	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
202	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. If you don't have a local `references/<file>.md` containing the source text, dispatch a retriever and wait. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.
203	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.
204	
205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
244	
245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266	
267	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
268	
269	## Workflow
270	
271	1. Read your directive completely.
272	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
273	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
274	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
275	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
276	6. Make the edits. 1	\chapter{The tautological quotient and the universal property of $\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)$}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianQuot}
3	
4	The previous chapter constructed the Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
5	\(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing the affine charts \(U^I\)
6	(\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) along the transition isomorphisms
7	(\cref{def:gr_transition}) satisfying the cocycle condition
8	(\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}), and proved that the structure morphism
9	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated})
10	and proper (\cref{lem:gr_proper}). This chapter adds the next layer: the
11	\emph{tautological rank-\(d\) quotient}
12	\(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of the
13	trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf, and the \emph{universal property} that makes
14	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) the fine moduli space of rank-\(d\) locally free quotients of
15	\(\mathcal{O}^{r}\) -- equivalently, the representing scheme of the Quot functor
16	of the trivial sheaf.
17	
18	Throughout, \(r \ge d \ge 1\) are the fixed integers of the previous chapter, and
19	for a size-\(d\) subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) we write \(R^I\) for the
20	chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) of \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) and \(R^I_J\) for the
21	localisation along \(P^I_J = \det(X^I_J)\) defining the overlap
22	\(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\).
23	
24	\section{Infrastructure: locally free sheaves and module gluing}
25	\label{sec:grquot_infra}
26	
27	The constructions of this chapter -- the universal quotient sheaf
28	\(\mathcal{U}\), the tautological quotient \(u\), and the functor of points -- rest
29	on two pieces of foundational machinery for sheaves of modules that are not
30	available off the shelf and are therefore set up here. The first is the predicate
31	``locally free of rank \(d\)'', which records that a sheaf of modules is, on the
32	members of some open cover, isomorphic to the trivial bundle \(\mathcal{O}^{d}\);
33	it is the condition cut out in the definition of the Grassmannian functor. The
34	second is a gluing primitive that descends a sheaf of modules (and a morphism of
35	such sheaves) from per-chart data along an open cover, given a transition cocycle
36	compatible with the cover's own gluing; it is the vehicle that assembles the
37	per-chart free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}^{d}_{U^I}\) and the per-chart quotients
38	\(u^I\) into the global \(\mathcal{U}\) and \(u\).
39	
40	\begin{definition}
41	[Locally free of rank \(d\)]
42	  \label{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
43	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank}
44	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{M}\) a sheaf of
45	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
46	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). We say \(\mathcal{M}\) is \emph{locally free of rank
47	  \(d\)} if there exists an open cover \(\{U_i\}_{i}\) of \(X\) -- a family of
48	  opens with \(\bigcup_i U_i = X\) -- such that for each index \(i\) the
49	  restriction \(\mathcal{M}|_{U_i}\) of \(\mathcal{M}\) to \(U_i\) is isomorphic,
50	  as a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules, to the trivial rank-\(d\) bundle
51	  \[
52	    \mathcal{M}|_{U_i} \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_{U_i}^{\,d}
53	      \;=\; \bigoplus_{k \in \{1,\dots,d\}} \mathcal{O}_{U_i},
54	  \]
55	  the free sheaf on the index set of size \(d\). This is the analogue, for the
56	  rank-\(d\) free model, of the ``open cover together with a local isomorphism''
57	  shape used to express quasi-coherence and local finite presentation: it
58	  asserts the existence of a trivialising cover on which \(\mathcal{M}\) acquires
59	  a basis of \(d\) sections. A sheaf that is locally free of rank \(d\) is in
60	  particular quasi-coherent and locally of finite presentation, and its fibre at
61	  every point is a \(d\)-dimensional vector space. This is the condition imposed
62	  on the member sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) in a family of rank-\(d\) quotients
63	  (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}); the universal sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\)
64	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}) satisfies it tautologically, with the
65	  charts \(\{U^I\}\) as trivialising cover.
66	\end{definition}
67	
68	\begin{definition}
69	[Gluing a sheaf of modules along an open cover]
70	  \label{def:scheme_modules_glue}
71	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glue}
72	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data}
73	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}), with index set \(J\), charts \(U_i\),
74	  pairwise overlaps \(V_{ij}\), open immersions
75	  \(f_{ij} : V_{ij} \hookrightarrow U_i\), and transition isomorphisms
76	  \(t_{ij} : V_{ij} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{ji}\) satisfying the glue datum's
77	  cocycle conditions; write \(X\) for the glued scheme and
78	  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\) for the canonical open immersions of the
79	  charts. Suppose given:
80	  \begin{itemize}
81	    \item for each \(i \in J\), a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}_i\) on the chart \(U_i\);
82	    \item for each pair \((i,j)\), a \emph{transition isomorphism} of sheaves of
83	      modules on the overlap \(V_{ij}\),
84	      \[
85	        g_{ij} \;:\; f_{ij}^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
86	          \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
87	        (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j,
88	      \]
89	      comparing the two charts' sheaves after restriction (pullback) to the
90	      common overlap \(V_{ij}\);
91	  \end{itemize}
92	  subject to the \emph{module cocycle conditions} compatible with \(D\): the
93	  self-transition \(g_{ii}\) is the identity, and over each triple overlap the
94	  composite \(g_{jk} \circ g_{ij}\) agrees with \(g_{ik}\) once transported
95	  through the scheme-level triple transition of \(D\) (the multiplicative,
96	  \(\mathrm{GL}_d\)-valued cocycle condition for the family
97	  \((g_{ij})\)). Out of this data the construction produces:
98	  \begin{enumerate}
99	    \item a glued sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}\) on the glued scheme \(X\); and
100	    \item for each \(i \in J\) a \emph{restriction isomorphism}
101	      \(\rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\,\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}_i\)
102	      identifying the pullback of \(\mathcal{M}\) along \(\iota_i\) with the
103	      \(i\)-th chart sheaf, the \(\rho_i\) being compatible with the transition
104	      isomorphisms \(g_{ij}\) over the overlaps.
105	  \end{enumerate}
106	  The pair \((\mathcal{M}, (\rho_i)_i)\) is characterised up to unique
107	  isomorphism by the restriction property: \(\mathcal{M}\) is the unique sheaf of
108	  modules on \(X\) (up to canonical isomorphism) restricting to each
109	  \(\mathcal{M}_i\) compatibly with \((g_{ij})\). The same primitive descends
110	  morphisms: given two such glued data \((\mathcal{M}_i, g_{ij})\) and
111	  \((\mathcal{N}_i, h_{ij})\) with glued sheaves \(\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}\), and
112	  a family of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-linear morphisms
113	  \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\) that commute with the transition
114	  isomorphisms on every overlap, there is a unique morphism
115	  \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) of sheaves of modules on \(X\) with
116	  \(\rho_i^{\mathcal{N}} \circ \iota_i^{*}\phi = \phi_i \circ \rho_i^{\mathcal{M}}\)
117	  for every \(i\) -- the descent of \(\mathrm{Hom}\) along the cover.
118	
119	  \emph{Construction.} A sheaf of modules on \(X\) is equivalent to a compatible
120	  family of sheaves on the members of an open cover of \(X\) together with
121	  identifications on the overlaps -- effective descent for the Zariski cover
122	  \(\{\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\}\). The charts \(U_i\), being the images of
123	  the glue datum's objects, form such a cover, with intersections
124	  \(U_i \cap U_j\) identified with the overlaps \(V_{ij}\) via the structural data
125	  of \(D\). Pulling the sections of an abstract \(\mathcal{M}\) back to each chart
126	  must reproduce \(\mathcal{M}_i\); the transition isomorphisms \(g_{ij}\),
127	  satisfying the cocycle conditions, are exactly the descent datum that makes the
128	  family \((\mathcal{M}_i)\) glue, so the descent produces \(\mathcal{M}\) with
129	  the asserted restriction isomorphisms \(\rho_i\). The morphism statement is the
130	  descent of morphisms for the same cover: a morphism on \(X\) is a family of
131	  morphisms on the charts agreeing on overlaps, and the compatibility of the
132	  \(\phi_i\) with \((g_{ij}, h_{ij})\) is precisely the agreement condition,
133	  giving the unique glued \(\phi\). This specialises the general descent of sheaves
134	  of modules to the concrete open cover supplied by a scheme glue datum,
135	  and is the form in which the Grassmannian's tautological bundle and quotient are
136	  built.
137	\end{definition}
138	
139	\section{The tautological quotient on the charts}
140	\label{sec:grquot_chart}
141	
142	\begin{definition}
143	[Chart quotient \(u^I\)]
144	  \label{def:gr_chart_quotient}
145	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap}
146	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix}
147	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
148	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
149	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L898-L910).
150	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
151	  % "We next define a rank $d$ locally free sheaf $\U$ on $\grass(r,d)$
152	  %  together with a surjective homomorphism $\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$.
153	  %  On each $U^I$ we define a surjective homomorphism
154	  %  $u^I :\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I} \to  \oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
155	  %  by the matrix $X^I$."
156	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
157	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). On the affine chart
158	  \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) define the
159	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}\)-linear homomorphism of trivial bundles
160	  \[
161	    u^I \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}
162	      \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}
163	  \]
164	  given by left multiplication by the universal \(d \times r\) matrix \(X^I\)
165	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}): on global sections it is the \(R^I\)-linear
166	  map \((R^I)^{r} \to (R^I)^{d}\), \(v \mapsto X^I v\). Since the \(I\)-th minor
167	  \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\) is the identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\), the composite of
168	  \(u^I\) with the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates
169	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \hookrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) is the
170	  identity; hence \(u^I\) is a split surjection of locally free sheaves, with
171	  target the free rank-\(d\) sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\).
172	\end{definition}
173	
174	\begin{definition}
175	[The universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\)]
176	  \label{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}
177	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalQuotient}
178	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universalMinorInv, def:gr_transition,
179	    lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:scheme_modules_glue,
180	    def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
181	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
182	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
183	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L903-L910).
184	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
185	  % "Compatible with the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$
186	  %  for gluing the affine pieces $U^I$, we give gluing data
187	  %  $(g_{I,J})$ for gluing together the trivial bundles $\oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
188	  %  by putting
189	  %  $$g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I_J)$$"
190	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
191	  Over each overlap \(U^I_J = \Spec R^I_J\) define the bundle-transition matrix
192	  \[
193	    g_{I,J} \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} \;\in\; \mathrm{GL}_d(R^I_J),
194	  \]
195	  the Cramer inverse of the localised \(J\)-minor (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}),
196	  which is invertible because \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit of \(R^I_J\). The matrices
197	  \((g_{I,J})\) are compatible with the chart-gluing cocycle \((\theta_{I,J})\)
198	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{lem:gr_cocycle}): over a triple overlap they
199	  satisfy \(g_{I,K} = g_{J,K}\, g_{I,J}\) and \(g_{I,I} = 1\), the multiplicative
200	  cocycle condition for a vector bundle. Gluing the free rank-\(d\) sheaves
201	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) along the isomorphisms \(g_{I,J}\) over the cover
202	  \(\{U^I\}\) of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) therefore
203	  produces a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-module
204	  \(\mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). Its determinant line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\)
205	  has transition functions \(\det(g_{I,J}) = 1/P^I_J\), the data underlying the
206	  Pl\"ucker embedding.
207	\end{definition}
208	
209	\begin{definition}
210	[The tautological quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}^{r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\)]
211	  \label{def:tautological_quotient}
212	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.tautologicalQuotient}
213	  \uses{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_chart_quotient,
214	    def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_cocycle,
215	    def:scheme_modules_glue}
216	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
217	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
218	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L908-L910).
219	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
220	  % "This is compatible with the homomorphisms $u^I$,
221	  %  so we get a surjective homomorphism
222	  %  $u : \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$."
223	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
224	  The chart quotients \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
225	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) are compatible with the bundle gluing
226	  \((g_{I,J})\) of \(\mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}): over
227	  each overlap \(U^I_J\) one has the identity of \(d \times r\) matrices
228	  \[
229	    g_{I,J}\, X^I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I \;=\; X^J \qquad \text{on } U^I_J,
230	  \]
231	  which is precisely the defining formula of the chart transition
232	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}).
233	  Hence the local maps \(u^I\), transported through the trivialisations of
234	  \(\mathcal{U}\), agree on overlaps and glue to a single surjective
235	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-linear homomorphism
236	  \[
237	    u \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r}
238	      \;\twoheadrightarrow\; \mathcal{U},
239	  \]
240	  the \emph{tautological} (or \emph{universal}) rank-\(d\) quotient of the trivial
241	  rank-\(r\) sheaf. It is surjective because each \(u^I\) is (split) surjective and
242	  surjectivity is local on \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and \(\mathcal{U}\) is locally
243	  free of rank \(d\) by \cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}. The pair
244	  \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is the family of rank-\(d\) quotients carried
245	  by \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) itself.
246	\end{definition}
247	
248	\section{The functor of points}
249	\label{sec:grquot_functor}
250	
251	\begin{definition}
252	[The Grassmannian / Quot-of-trivial-sheaf functor \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\)]
253	  \label{def:grassmannian_functor}
254	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.functor}
255	  \uses{def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:quot_functor}
256	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
257	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
258	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L557-L564).
259	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
260	  % "represents the
261	  %  contravariant functor
262	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
263	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
264	  %  set of all equivalence classes
265	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
266	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$."
267	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
268	  The \emph{Grassmannian functor}
269	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d) = \Quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}
270	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/\mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) is the contravariant functor
271	  from schemes to sets defined as follows. For a scheme \(T\), a \emph{family of
272	  rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} is a pair
273	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a locally free
274	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) of rank \(d\) together with a surjective
275	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-linear homomorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
276	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\). Two such families
277	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) and \(\langle \mathcal{F}', q' \rangle\) are
278	  \emph{equivalent} if there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F}
279	  \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with \(f \circ q = q'\); equivalently
280	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\) as subsheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). Set
281	  \[
282	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)
283	      \;=\; \bigl\{\, \text{equivalence classes } \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle
284	        \text{ of rank-}d\text{ quotients of } \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \,\bigr\}.
285	  \]
286	  For a morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\), the map
287	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(\varphi)\) sends \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\)
288	  to the pulled-back quotient \(\langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{F},\, \varphi^{*}q
289	  \rangle\); this is well-defined on equivalence classes because pullback is right
290	  exact and preserves local freeness of rank \(d\). (As \(\mathcal{F}\) is locally
291	  free of rank \(d\), surjectivity is preserved and the support is automatically
292	  proper -- all of \(T'\) -- so this is the special case
293	  \(E = \mathcal{O}^{r}\), \(X = S = \operatorname{base}\) of the general Quot
294	  functor of \cref{def:quot_functor}.)
295	\end{definition}
296	
297	\section{The universal property}
298	\label{sec:grquot_universal}
299	
300	\begin{theorem}
301	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) represents the Grassmannian functor]
302	  \label{thm:grassmannian_universal_property}
303	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.represents}
304	  \uses{def:grassmannian_functor, def:tautological_quotient,
305	    def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_affine_chart,
306	    def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
307	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
308	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
309	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L566).
310	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
311	  % "Show that $\grass(r,d)$ together with the quotient
312	  %  $u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ represents the
313	  %  contravariant functor
314	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
315	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
316	  %  set of all equivalence classes
317	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
318	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
319	  %  Therefore, $\quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$
320	  %  exists, and equals $\grass(r,d)$."
321	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
322	  The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}),
323	  together with the tautological quotient \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\)
324	  (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}), represents the functor
325	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}). That is, for
326	  every scheme \(T\) the natural map
327	  \[
328	    \operatorname{Hom}(T, \mathrm{Gr}(r,d))
329	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
330	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T),
331	    \qquad
332	    \bigl(\,\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\,\bigr)
333	      \;\longmapsto\;
334	    \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U},\, \varphi^{*}u \rangle,
335	  \]
336	  is a bijection, natural in \(T\). Consequently the Quot scheme
337	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
338	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) of rank-\(d\) quotients of the trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf
339	  exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\); the tautological quotient is the
340	  universal family.
341	\end{theorem}
342	
343	\begin{proof}
344	  \uses{def:tautological_quotient, def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf,
345	    def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix,
346	    def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme,
347	    def:grassmannian_functor}
348	  Naturality of \(\varphi \mapsto \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u
349	  \rangle\) is immediate: pullback is functorial and \(\varphi^{*}u\) is again a
350	  surjection onto a rank-\(d\) locally free sheaf, so the assignment defines a
351	  morphism of functors \(\operatorname{Hom}(-,\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)) \to
352	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\). It remains to show it is a bijection for each
353	  \(T\); we exhibit an inverse, constructed Zariski-locally on \(T\) and glued.
354	
355	  \emph{From a quotient to a morphism.} Let \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle \in
356	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)\), so \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow
357	  \mathcal{F}\) with \(\mathcal{F}\) locally free of rank \(d\). For a size-\(d\)
358	  subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) let \(T_I \subseteq T\) be the locus where
359	  the composite
360	  \[
361	    \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}
362	      \overset{\ \iota_I\ }{\hookrightarrow} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
363	      \xrightarrow{\ q\ } \mathcal{F},
364	  \]
365	  with \(\iota_I\) the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, is an
366	  isomorphism. This locus is open: in a local trivialisation of \(\mathcal{F}\)
367	  the composite is a \(d \times d\) matrix and \(T_I\) is the non-vanishing locus
368	  of its determinant. The \(T_I\) cover \(T\): at any point \(t\), the
369	  \(d\)-dimensional fibre quotient \(q(t) : \kappa(t)^{r} \twoheadrightarrow
370	  \mathcal{F}\otimes\kappa(t)\) is surjective, so some \(d\) of the \(r\) standard
371	  basis vectors map to a basis of the \(d\)-dimensional fibre, i.e.\ \(t \in T_I\)
372	  for that \(I\) (by Nakayama the isomorphism then holds in a neighbourhood).
373	
374	  On \(T_I\), trivialise \(\mathcal{F}\) using \(\iota_I^{*}\): the quotient
375	  becomes a surjection \(q_I : \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,d}\)
376	  represented by a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M^I\) whose \(I\)-th minor \(M^I_I\) is
377	  the identity \(1_{d \times d}\) -- exactly the shape of the universal matrix
378	  \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}). The \(d(r-d)\) free entries of \(M^I\)
379	  are global functions on \(T_I\) and hence determine a unique
380	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism \(R^I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to
381	  \Gamma(T_I, \mathcal{O}_{T_I})\) sending \(x^I_{p,q}\) to the corresponding entry
382	  of \(M^I\), i.e.\ a unique morphism \(\varphi_I : T_I \to U^I\)
383	  (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) with \(\varphi_I^{*}X^I = M^I\) and thus
384	  \(\varphi_I^{*}u^I = q_I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}).
385	
386	  \emph{Gluing.} On the overlap \(T_I \cap T_J\) the two trivialisations differ by
387	  the change-of-basis \((M^I_J)^{-1}\), which is exactly the bundle transition
388	  \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) of \(\mathcal{U}\)
389	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}); concretely
390	  \(M^J = (M^I_J)^{-1} M^I = \varphi_I^{*}\theta_{I,J}(X^J)\)
391	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}). Hence
392	  \(\varphi_I\) and \(\varphi_J\) land in the overlap charts and agree there after
393	  the transition \(\theta_{I,J}\), so by the cocycle condition
394	  (\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}) the \(\varphi_I\) glue to a single morphism
395	  \(\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}). By
396	  construction \(\varphi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F},
397	  q \rangle\), since this holds on each \(T_I\) and the equivalence class is
398	  determined by \(\ker(q)\).
399	
400	  \emph{Uniqueness.} If \(\psi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) also satisfies
401	  \(\psi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), then
402	  on \(\psi^{-1}(U^I)\) the pulled-back tautological quotient \(\psi^{*}u^I\)
403	  equals \(q\) in the \(I\)-trivialisation, which forces \(\psi^{*}X^I = M^I\) and
404	  hence \(\psi = \varphi_I\) there; as the \(U^I\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\),
405	  \(\psi = \varphi\). Thus \(\varphi\) is the unique morphism inducing
406	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), and \(\varphi \mapsto \langle
407	  \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u \rangle\) is bijective.
408	
409	  Therefore \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is a fine
410	  moduli space: it represents \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\), so
411	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
412	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
413	\end{proof}

giving the unique glued \(\phi\). This specialises the general descent of sheaves
  of modules to the concrete open cover supplied by a scheme glue datum,
  and is the form in which the Grassmannian's tautological bundle and quotient are
  built.

  \emph{Realisation (construction path).} There is no turn-key gluing of sheaves
  of modules over a scheme glue datum available off the shelf; the construction
  below is original to this development, and it is the most involved declaration of
  the chapter -- expect a multi-step build assembled from the primitives named here,
  not an appeal to a single ready-made result. Concretely, instantiate \(D\) as the
  Grassmannian glue datum (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}), with glued scheme
  \(X = \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) and its canonical open cover by the charts
  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\). The glued sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\) is built from
  the per-chart sheaves \(\mathcal{M}_i\) and the overlap transition isomorphisms
  \(g_{ij}\) satisfying the module cocycle conditions, and is pinned down by the
  restriction isomorphisms \(\rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim}
  \mathcal{M}_i\) compatible with the \(g_{ij}\). Restriction of a sheaf of modules
  to a chart is computed through the geometric restriction bridge of the
  development: for the open immersion \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\), the
  pullback \(\iota_i^{*}\) realises an equivalence between the sheaves of modules on
  \(X\) seen over the open \(U_i\) and the sheaves of modules on the open subscheme
  \(U_i\) itself -- the same open-immersion pullback restriction equivalence used to
  descend quasi-coherence along a basic open in the Quot-scheme construction. The
  transition isomorphisms \(g_{ij}\), forming a cocycle for this cover, constitute a
  descent datum; gluing the local sections then yields \(\mathcal{M}\) together with
  the restriction isomorphisms \(\rho_i\), where both the assembly and the
  characterisation up to unique isomorphism rest on the locality of sections of a
  sheaf -- a compatible family of sections over the cover, agreeing on the overlaps,
  extends to a unique global section.

  The morphism-descent half is the same locality argument applied to homomorphisms:
  a family \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\) commuting with the transition
  isomorphisms over every overlap is a compatible family of local morphisms, and the
  uniqueness of gluing of sections promotes it to a unique global
  \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) of sheaves of modules on \(X\) restricting
  to each \(\phi_i\) through the \(\rho_i\).
\end{definition}
